The offer is anchored slightly above the observed market reference to protect supplier margin while leaving space for negotiation.

Thanks for the opening proposal. That level is well outside what we can support for this program, so if there’s a path forward it needs to start much closer to our cost constraints and volume economics. I can open at 2.57 per unit today, and if you can pair that with reliable supply and forecast alignment, we can keep the conversation moving quickly. The supplier's 6.74 offer is far above our buying limit, so accepting is not viable. A low counteroffer preserves our margin and signals that the current gap is too large without ending the negotiation immediately.

I appreciate the quick response, but 2.57 is still far below a workable level for us given supply costs and program commitment. We can move meaningfully from our prior position and offer 6.12 per unit, which reflects a real concession while keeping the supply side sustainable. If you have flexibility to move up substantially, we can continue narrowing the gap. The buyer's offer is only one cent above my floor, but accepting it would leave almost no margin and is far below both my prior ask and the latest market reference. A counteroffer keeps the negotiation alive, signals willingness to concede, and anchors closer to favorable pricing while there is still room in later rounds.

Thanks for the movement, but 6.12 per unit is still far outside what we can support on our side for this program. Even with the broader market running higher, our procurement ceiling and downstream margin requirements leave no path to agreement anywhere near that level, so continuing against this offer would not be productive. If your economics change materially and you can come back much closer to the high-2s, we could revisit.
